planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full shade; bloody dock thrives in shaded woodland gardens and is one of the most ornamental leafy greens for shaded spots, producing glossy, dark-green leaves with vivid crimson-red veins and midribs.

directions: Direct sow in early spring or fall in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; thin to 12 inches. Rumex sanguineus (bloody dock or red-veined sorrel) is a European perennial dock producing some of the most visually striking leaves of any vegetable — glossy, lance-shaped, medium-green leaves dramatically marked with vivid crimson-red veins and midribs, creating a graphic color contrast that makes this plant as ornamental as it is edible. Young leaves are harvested like sorrel and used raw in salads, where the mild, slightly acidic flavor adds interest; older leaves become more strongly acidic (from oxalic acid) and are best cooked briefly like spinach or used sparingly as a flavoring. Unlike common garden sorrel, the leaf flavor is milder and the visual interest much greater. Plants are long-lived perennials that die back in hard winters and regrow in spring; in zone 9, they may persist semi-evergreen through mild winters. The tall, reddish flower stalks should be removed as they develop to keep plants producing leaves rather than going to seed. Harvest outer leaves regularly to encourage continued production.
sources: University of Minnesota Extension — Edible Leafy Greens

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; Japanese anemone is slow to establish in its first year but spreads vigorously by rhizome once settled, producing magnificent late-season blooms in August–October when most shade perennials have finished flowering.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade; avoid disturbing the roots once established — plants resent transplanting. Anemone hupehensis (Japanese anemone) is one of the most valuable shade-garden perennials for late-season bloom — a Chinese native that fills the August–October gap when spring-blooming shade perennials have long finished, producing elegant, saucer-shaped, 2-to-3-inch flowers with five or more silky petals in pink, rose, or white surrounding a central cluster of golden stamens, on tall, branching, wiry stems rising 2–4 feet above the bold, dark-green, lobed basal foliage. The flowers have a gossamer, butterfly-like quality and sway gracefully in the breeze; they are excellent for cutting. Plants spread gradually by underground rhizomes to form expanding colonies — welcome in a large shade garden, potentially aggressive in a small one. The species Anemone hupehensis differs from the hybrid A. × hybrida in being slightly smaller and less vigorous; cultivars like 'September Charm' (single pink) and 'Pamina' (semi-double deep pink) are widely grown. Best in zone 9 if given afternoon shade and consistent moisture.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Shade Perennials

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions or container stock in spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade to part shade; ajuga is one of the most reliable and ornamental groundcovers for shaded areas under trees and along shaded borders, spreading by stolons to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to part shade; space 12–18 inches apart and allow to spread. Ajuga reptans (bugleweed or carpet bugleweed) is one of the most widely grown and adaptable shade groundcovers in temperate gardens — a European native that spreads by horizontal stolons to form a dense, 4-to-6-inch-tall, weed-suppressing carpet of rosettes, producing spectacular spikes of small, tubular, blue-violet (or pink, white, or cream in cultivars) flowers in April–May that are important early-season nectar sources for native bees and bumble bees. The foliage is the primary season-long attraction: many cultivars offer striking leaf colors from dark bronze-purple ('Catlin's Giant,' 'Burgundy Glow') to variegated cream and pink ('Burgundy Glow') to metallic chocolate ('Black Scallop'). Ajuga thrives in the difficult dry-shade conditions under large trees where other groundcovers struggle. It is essentially maintenance-free once established, spreading moderately (not invasively) by stolons. In zone 9, it performs best in shade with consistent moisture and may go partially dormant in the hottest, driest summers; crown rot can occur in hot, humid conditions — ensure good air circulation.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Shade Groundcovers

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade to part shade; sweetbox is one of the most elegant and fragrant small shrubs for shaded gardens, thriving under high-canopy trees where few shrubs succeed.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade to part shade; avoid dry soil or full sun, which bleaches and scorches the leaves. Sarcococca hookeriana (Himalayan sweetbox) is one of the finest shade shrubs for temperate and mild-winter gardens — a compact, slowly spreading, Himalayan evergreen shrub producing small, glossy, narrow, lance-shaped, dark-green leaves on gracefully arching stems, and tiny, thread-like, white flowers of almost no visual impact but extraordinary fragrance in January–March, filling the winter air with an intense, vanilla-like perfume that is among the most powerful and pleasing of any garden plant in bloom. The flowers are followed by small, glossy, black drupes (berries) attractive to birds. Plants spread slowly by rhizomes to form tidy, weed-suppressing colonies. The variety humilis (dwarf form, to 24 inches) is lower and more spreading; var. digyna is slightly taller with narrower leaves. Sweetbox is superbly adapted to the dry shade and root competition under large trees — one of the most underused shrubs for difficult shaded spots. In zone 9, protect from afternoon sun and provide reliable summer moisture.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Shade Shrubs

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, strongly to moderately acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in part shade; mountain pieris is a native Appalachian shrub for the upper South and is more cold-hardy and less heat-tolerant than its Japanese cousin (P. japonica) — best in zone 9 in morning sun with afternoon shade.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly to moderately acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), well-drained soil in part shade; mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and acidity; avoid hot, dry, alkaline conditions. Pieris floribunda (mountain pieris or mountain fetterbush) is a native evergreen shrub of the high Appalachian Mountains of Virginia through Georgia — the North American counterpart to the widely grown Japanese pieris (P. japonica) — producing upright clusters (panicles) of small, white, urn-shaped flowers in March–April at the branch tips, with the characteristic red-to-bronze new growth that distinguishes the genus. Unlike the pendulous flower clusters of Japanese pieris, P. floribunda produces erect, branching flower panicles. The shrub is more compact and slower-growing than Japanese pieris, reaching 2–6 feet in maturity, and is somewhat more tolerant of deer browse. It is an excellent native alternative to P. japonica for acidic-soil woodland and shade gardens in the upper and mid-South. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested — it is one of the classic poisonous woodland shrubs of the southern Appalachians. Note: in the lower South and zone 9, Japanese pieris (P. japonica) typically performs better in the heat.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Native Shrubs
